title: Chambly Basin

infobox.value: (clockwise) ruisseau Wilson, [rivière des Hurons](/wiki/Rivi%C3%A8re_des_Hurons_(Richelieu_River_tributary) "Rivière des Hurons (Richelieu River tributary)"), [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River"), [Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal")

(French: *bassin de Chambly*, pronounced [[basɛ̃ də ʃɑ̃bli]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is a shallow, oblong [body of water](/wiki/Waterbody "Waterbody") formed by an enlargement of the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River"), extending out from the foot of the [rapids](/wiki/Rapids "Rapids") on the river between [Chambly](/wiki/Chambly,_Quebec "Chambly, Quebec") and [Richelieu](/wiki/Richelieu,_Quebec "Richelieu, Quebec"), in the [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") region of [Montérégie](/wiki/Mont%C3%A9r%C3%A9gie "Montérégie"). The towns of Chambly, Richelieu, [Saint-Mathias-sur-Richelieu](/wiki/Saint-Mathias-sur-Richelieu "Saint-Mathias-sur-Richelieu") and [Carignan](/wiki/Carignan,_Quebec "Carignan, Quebec") surround the basin.

[Atlas of Canada from the Department of Natural Resources Canada - Characteristics extracted from the geographic map, the database and site instrumentation - accessed May 18, 2020.](http://atlas.gc.ca/toporama/en/index.html)

[Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain") (2018-05-04) [1613]. "VIII". [*Oeuvres de Champlain*](https://www.gutenberg.org/files/17258/17258-h/17258-h.htm) [*Works of Champlain*]. Vol. Tome III, Livre Second. p. 181. Retrieved 2022-01-07.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Body of water in Montérégie, Quebec

For other uses, see [Chambly (disambiguation)](/wiki/Chambly_(disambiguation) "Chambly (disambiguation)").

The **Chambly Basin** (French: *bassin de Chambly*, pronounced [[basɛ̃ də ʃɑ̃bli]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is a shallow, oblong [body of water](/wiki/Waterbody "Waterbody") formed by an enlargement of the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River"), extending out from the foot of the [rapids](/wiki/Rapids "Rapids") on the river between [Chambly](/wiki/Chambly,_Quebec "Chambly, Quebec") and [Richelieu](/wiki/Richelieu,_Quebec "Richelieu, Quebec"), in the [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") region of [Montérégie](/wiki/Mont%C3%A9r%C3%A9gie "Montérégie"). The towns of Chambly, Richelieu, [Saint-Mathias-sur-Richelieu](/wiki/Saint-Mathias-sur-Richelieu "Saint-Mathias-sur-Richelieu") and [Carignan](/wiki/Carignan,_Quebec "Carignan, Quebec") surround the basin.

The Chambly Basin is an important area for recreational tourism activities including boating in the summer, the connected [Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal") and nearby [Fort Chambly](/wiki/Fort_Chambly "Fort Chambly") [National Historic Sites](/wiki/National_Historic_Site_of_Canada "National Historic Site of Canada"). Its surface is generally frozen from mid-December to the end of March. The water level of the river varies with the seasons and the precipitation.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly_Basin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

The shape of the basin is similar to the shape of a football. The current from the [Richelieu River](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River") arrives from the southeast by crossing the Chambly rapids for a length of approximately 1.35 kilometres (0.84 mi), i.e. downstream from the Yule bridge which spans this river in order to connect Chambly (west bank) and [Richelieu](/wiki/Richelieu,_Quebec "Richelieu, Quebec") (east bank). These rapids end at the height of an archipelago of small islands bordering the south-eastern shore of the basin.

The [Rivière des Hurons](/wiki/Rivi%C3%A8re_des_Hurons_(Richelieu_River_tributary) "Rivière des Hurons (Richelieu River tributary)") flows toward the middle of the eastern shore of the basin. The [Chambly Canal](/wiki/Chambly_Canal "Chambly Canal") pours its waters on the south shore of the basin, in the area of the Chambly Canal National Historic Site of Canada.

The western part of the basin includes [Île aux Lièvres](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_aux_Li%C3%A8vres_(Richelieu_River) "Île aux Lièvres (Richelieu River)"), [Demers Island](/wiki/Demers_Island "Demers Island") (residential area), Île au Foin and [Goyer Island](/wiki/Goyer_Island "Goyer Island") (residential area). Channels separate these islands. These islands are part of the territory of the city of Carignan. The [Acadia River](/wiki/Acadia_River "Acadia River") which runs on the west side of Chambly drains at the northeast point of [Goyer Island](/wiki/Goyer_Island "Goyer Island"), that is 1.0 kilometre (0.62 mi) downstream of the limit north of the Chambly basin.[[1]](#cite_note-location-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly_Basin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain"), the European to explore this body water in [1609](/wiki/1609 "1609"), wrote: "The entrance to the sault is a kind of lake, where the water goes down, which contains some three [leagues](/wiki/League_(unit) "League (unit)") round."
[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In [Jesuit Relations](/wiki/Jesuit_Relations "Jesuit Relations") of [1665](/wiki/1665 "1665"), we read: "This basin is like a small lake, a league and a half of a turn, six to eight feet deep.[[3]](#cite_note-3) "

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chambly_Basin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Name")]

Chambly Basin was named after the surrounding [seignory](/wiki/Seigneurial_system_of_New_France "Seigneurial system of New France") of Chambly. It's referred to as a "basin" as it's a shallow lake in which alluvium is deposited.[[4]](#cite_note-CTQ-4) The name French: *Bassin de Chambly* was formalized by the Quebec [Commission of Geography](/w/index.php?title=Commission_of_Geography&action=edit&redlink=1 "Commission of Geography (page does not exist)") in 1968.
